channel.title: Combined Economist RSS Feed
channel.description: Combined feed of multiple Economist RSS sources with archive.is/o/nuunc links

item.title: American corporate profits keep shrugging off global tumult
item.description: Earnings expectations are through the roof

item.title: Albertans find it harder than expected to break from Canada. Good
item.description: Separatists in the oil-rich province are beset by political and legal challenges

item.title: Chernobyl’s forgotten nuclear lessons
item.description: Forty years after the disaster, the power plant still poses a threat

item.title: The world wants Chinese tech. China is determined to keep it
item.description: China’s rivals are learning how to get what China won’t share

item.title: Anduril, Palantir and SpaceX are changing how America wages war
item.description: The Trump administration is cosying up to a clique of “neo-primes”

item.title: How Chinese satellites have boosted Iran’s war effort
item.description: As American images went dark, Chinese alternatives filled the void

item.title: Which Iran is America dealing with?
item.description: Rivalries in the Iranian leadership could stymie a truce

item.title: What have the Mughals ever done for us?
item.description: How India’s greatest Muslim empire built its most powerful Hindu party

item.title: Why your AI assistant is suddenly selling to you
item.description: Sponsored chats are changing the way digital advertising works

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Of God and MAGA
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our executive editor and New York bureau chief, unpacks the blurring of church and state among Donald Trump’s circle

item.title: Hormuz is (apparently) unblocked. Energy markets remain a mess
item.description: Mines, mistrust and missing ships will keep markets tight for months

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The Mythos moment
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Is bone broth good for you?
item.description: Or is it just tasty?

item.title: A lasting Lebanese peace still looks a long way off
item.description: Donald Trump has enforced a ceasefire, but that is just a start

item.title: Why eldest siblings are brainier
item.description: A new study finds that sickness may play a role

item.title: Tumour cells use a genetic trick to become drug-resistant
item.description: The same trick could also be used against them

item.title: Venezuela is not the triumph Donald Trump claims, but it’s improving
item.description: Economic growth may accelerate and the regime has become less repressive

item.title: The impending global food shock is preventable
item.description: So why won’t it be prevented?

item.title: Donald Trump has made Venezuela a better place
item.description: But the American president is misinterpreting an incomplete success

item.title: Peter Magyar’s victory will keep Hungary in the spotlight
item.description: The country will become a test case for reversing democratic decay

item.title: Singapore and Malaysia knock heads over the war in Iran
item.description: They have their own strait to worry about

item.title: Soaring numbers of Chinese women demand divorce
item.description: They are defying state efforts to make it harder

item.title: Training for Beijing’s humanoid half-marathon is gruelling
item.description: China is racing towards a robotic future

item.title: The game theory behind violating ceasefires
item.description: Truces like the one in the Gulf get broken all the time. But they still work

item.title: J.D. Vance’s theory of Trumpism is no match for the practice
item.description: This White House is a graveyard for grand strategies

item.title: POTUS v the Pope
item.description: Running short of new people to poke, the president has a go at God’s vicar

item.title: The big business of survival bunkers
item.description: Another sign that prepping has gone mainstream

item.title: The Hudson is now so clean that everyone can eat from it
item.description: Battery sashimi, anyone?

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Politics
Business
European policymakers fiddle with energy prices, again
item.description: By subsidising fuel, they are delaying adjustments in demand

item.title: Where should Pablo Picasso’s “Guernica” be allowed to hang?
item.description: Arguments over the painting’s rightful place hark back to ugly regional politics

item.title: A scramble ahead of France’s presidential election
item.description: A populist-right victory is possible, but not inevitable

item.title: “Complete change of regime” in Hungary
item.description: Peter Magyar vows to root out corruption

item.title: The desperate pursuit of final approval
item.description: An office parable

item.title: Australia’s startup scene is thriving at last
item.description: Can the Lucky Country recreate Silicon Valley down under?

item.title: A victory in Benin’s presidential election was hardly democratic
item.description: One of west Africa’s bastions of democracy has fallen

item.title: Is bombing power plants and oil facilities a war crime?
item.description: The answer is more complicated than it seems

item.title: The Gulf war has settled into an uneasy limbo
item.description: Fighting is paused, Hormuz is shut and prospects for a deal are uncertain

item.title: Rio de Janeiro is a beautiful warning to the rest of Brazil
item.description: Local politicians are too often corrupt and criminal

item.title: Collapsing remittances will compound Asia’s energy shock
item.description: Could workers kicked out of the Gulf find fortune in East Asia?

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The ugly history of “America First”
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, revisits a moment of fierce isolationist sentiment

item.title: Could AI’s leading men become as powerful as Ford or Rockefeller?
item.description: For now, they are still a long way behind

item.title: Millions will go hungry if the Strait of Hormuz stays closed
item.description: Expensive fuel and fertiliser will lead to smaller harvests and higher prices

item.title: India’s space industry is blasting off
item.description: Thank Nehru, Modi and Musk

item.title: Introducing our Asia Bulletin newsletter
item.description: Every week, our team of correspondents will write about topics as varied as the region itself

item.title: Global imbalances are back. Who’s to blame?
item.description: The suspects look familiar

item.title: Pakistan’s deft diplomacy is an economic blessing. And a curse
item.description: It allows the country’s rulers to put off necessary reforms

item.title: America will come to regret its war on taxes
item.description: Lately, Democrats have joined the charge

item.title: Why China’s government worries about AI
item.description: Its concerns include jobs, security and beating America

item.title: America wakes up to AI’s dangerous power
item.description: After Mythos, a laissez-faire approach is no longer politically tenable or strategically wise

item.title: David Zaslav and the tyranny of incentives
item.description: The Warner CEO’s mega-payout is a cautionary tale

item.title: How to end the war in Iran
item.description: America and Iran must resume their efforts to strike a deal, even though it is sure to be an imperfect one

item.title: How natural selection really shaped humanity
item.description: Adaptation in response to evolutionary pressure is surprisingly common

item.title: Orban-proofing the EU
item.description: How the leader of a small, poor country held such sway over the bloc

item.title: Zimbabwe’s bizarre economic boom
item.description: A gold rush is combined with a power grab

item.title: How AI hackers will shake up cyber-security
item.description: The technology could eventually favour the defenders—but expect a bumpy ride

item.title: Pete Hegseth’s Pentagon is a lethality-maxxing wasps’ nest
item.description: America’s armed forces are supremely capable and roiled by infighting

item.title: Why Anthropic and OpenAI are locking up their latest models
item.description: Allowing only a privileged few companies to use them may make commercial sense

item.title: War will drain the Gulf’s $6trn treasure chest
item.description: The conflict complicates life for the custodians of Middle Eastern oil fortunes

item.title: Analysing Africa newsletter: A special edition answering your questions
item.description: John McDermott, our Chief Africa correspondent, rummages through the postbag

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: Decades under the smoke trail
item.description: Anshel Pfeffer, our Jerusalem correspondent, visits Kiryat Shmona, by the Israeli-Lebanese border

item.title: A tax revolt is under way in America
item.description: Republicans and Democrats alike want to take an axe to taxes

item.title: If it starts, a nuclear-arms race will be unstoppable
item.description: A sobering interview with the head of the International Atomic Energy Agency

item.title: How to build a portfolio when bonds fail to buffer stocks
item.description: The classic hedge has fallen apart, but don’t dump it just yet

item.title: A battle for female voters is changing India’s elections
item.description: Politicians are doling out cash to get their support

item.title: America’s corporate boards are under siege
item.description: Activist shareholders and regulatory changes are undermining their control

item.title: How to make buffet breakfasts less wasteful
item.description: A computer model has found some counterintuitive solutions

item.title: Israel and Lebanon both want to dislodge Hizbullah
item.description: Will Iran let them?

item.title: From Ralph Lauren to The Row, American luxury is booming
item.description: Can the old world fight back?

item.title: How Hungary can now lead the fight against illiberalism
item.description: Having vanquished Viktor Orban, Peter Magyar must get to work

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The consequences of the Hormuz blockade
item.description: Shashank Joshi, our defence editor, analyses what blocking off the strait would mean for oil prices, geopolitics and Donald Trump’s political calendar

item.title: In the Gulf, Ukraine flaunts its skill at intercepting drones
item.description: Deals with Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates show it is now a defence-industry power

item.title: Why the Trump administration is waging war on fraud
item.description: Medicare and Medicaid have problems. But that is not the whole story

item.title: Donald Trump’s blockade of Hormuz is a dangerous gamble
item.description: It further undermines international law and could lead to new fighting

item.title: China’s pension failings expose its harshest inequality
item.description: Uneven hand-outs see the rural elderly till the soil while urbanites enjoy retirement

item.title: The tech jobs bust is real. Don’t blame AI (yet)
item.description: Why technology firms are shedding workers

item.title: Peter Magyar topples illiberal hero Viktor Orban in Hungary
item.description: The upstart hands the populist a crushing defeat

item.title: America’s next Fed chair is caught in a vice
item.description: Inflation was heating up even before the war in Iran

item.title: The secret of India’s most liveable megacity
item.description: Kolkata’s pleasantness is not something to celebrate

item.title: There is still time to resurrect talks between America and Iran
item.description: But there is also a chance the war is about to get worse

item.title: America and Iran start a high-stakes negotiation to end their war
item.description: Their best hope may be an interim deal that buys time for more talks

item.title: The Climate Issue newsletter: The blue marble, then and now
item.description: Our planetary-affairs editor, Oliver Morton, looks at the connection between space travel and climate science

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Why Trump’s empty words still matter
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, reflects on the president’s threats to wipe out Iranian civilisation

item.title: After the war, how open will the Strait of Hormuz be?
item.description: Access may come at a price

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: Donald Trump is the war’s biggest loser
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Are sugar substitutes healthier than the real thing?
item.description: We share some bitter truths

item.title: Scam Inc has a new weapon
item.description: For the first time, investigators have traced spyware to its physical origin—revealing new types of duplicity

item.title: Even Hungary’s skewed elections might not save Viktor Orban
item.description: The ruling party is playing dirty. But our analysis shows the liberal opposition has a real chance of winning

item.title: Sir Keir Starmer is Britain’s best hope for legalising assisted dying
item.description: He should stop dawdling

item.title: Recriminations over Iran have heightened the risk of a break-up of NATO
item.description: Europe must prepare to defend itself

item.title: Cambodia honours a life-saving rat
item.description: The rodent helped clear more than 100 unexploded mines

item.title: China may be building a big new airbase in the South China Sea
item.description: But analysts are scratching their heads over the timing and location

item.title: AI micro-dramas are shaking up Chinese entertainment
item.description: Regulators threaten to spoil the fun

item.title: The West is doing more to combat China’s covert activity abroad
item.description: But China is paying little attention

item.title: Hospitals are stuck in a deadly doom loop
item.description: They never recovered from covid-19

item.title: That ugly ballroom epitomises the story of Donald Trump’s presidency
item.description: It illustrates his key means, methods, and goals, and reveals the true nature of his achievements

item.title: An environmentalist, a landowner and a libertarian walk into a barn
item.description: How a pipeline tangled political alliances in Iowa

item.title: Donald Trump’s ceasefire shows how America has changed
item.description: Contrast it with George Bush senior’s victory in Kuwait

item.title: Will California’s next governor be a fighter or a fixer?
item.description: Candidates with buzzy national profiles are leading those with local records

item.title: Business
The weekly cartoon
Politics
Earth and Moon, then and now
Sir Demis Hassabis wants to automate drug design
item.description: We speak to the boss of Google DeepMind

item.title: Why a big country like Italy acts as if it were small
item.description: Equivocating between Trump and Europe suits a history of switching sides

item.title: Viktor Orban is bashing Ukraine for votes
item.description: Hungary’s election poisons relations between Hungary and its neighbour

item.title: Europe’s joint nuclear-fusion project needs Russian expertise
item.description: At ITER Russian and Western scientists still work together—slowly

item.title: Most Syrians in Germany are there to stay
item.description: Sending back masses of immigrants would be foolish and impossible

item.title: The pros and cons of stretch goals
item.description: Setting really tough targets encourages risk-taking, for good and bad

item.title: Japan’s mighty carmakers are in serious trouble
item.description: They will need bold thinking to survive

item.title: A giant succession wave is coming for family businesses
item.description: Many will not survive

item.title: A deadly conflict in Malawi raises questions about conservation
item.description: How can humans coexist with wild animals?

item.title: Burkina Faso’s government is committing war crimes
item.description: While fighting jihadism, it has killed more civilians than the jihadists themselves

item.title: How anarchic was Africa?
item.description: Precolonial statelessness may have been a sign of success, not failure

item.title: Africa’s protests span countries, classes and causes
item.description: But can they bring lasting change?

item.title: There is little prospect of legalising abortion in Brazil
item.description: Other large countries in Latin America have either legalised or decriminalised

item.title: Some thoughts on prohibition
item.description: Our report from 1923

item.title: India’s religious minorities face harsher anti-conversion laws
item.description: Exhumations fuel communal tensions

item.title: How Pakistan emerged as an unlikely broker of peace in the Gulf
item.description: The troubled country has cannily managed its ties with the warring parties

item.title: America’s war on Iran has changed the Middle East—for the worse
item.description: This week’s ceasefire leaves the region less secure than when the war began

item.title: One neat trick to end extreme poverty
item.description: An age-old problem might have a surprisingly simple solution

item.title: Can the secondary market allay private-credit fears?
item.description: In the short run, a bit. In the long run, a lot more

item.title: The latest Italian banking whodunnit has it all
item.description: A a coup, a conspiracy and even a (possibly unrelated) corpse

item.title: South Korea’s AI industrial policy meets the energy shock
item.description: The collision will not be pretty

item.title: Donald Trump is the war’s biggest loser
item.description: There is a reason he wants an exit from Iran

item.title: How war has made a 33-year-old the Czech Republic’s richest man
item.description: The rapid rise of CSG, Europe’s newest defence giant

item.title: A ceasefire will not prevent the Iran war’s economic harm
item.description: Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens, expect lasting change to energy markets

item.title: The third Gulf war will scar energy markets for a long time yet
item.description: Residual risks and ruined infrastructure will keep prices high

item.title: Every company is now a media company—and every boss a star
item.description: The rise of the chatter-industrial complex

item.title: With the ceasefire looking shaky, the Gulf questions its future
item.description: Even as America and Iran pause, the war reveals how exposed the region has become

item.title: The war was steadily spiralling in scope and destruction
item.description: America, Israel and Iran were striking a widening array of targets across an expanding area

item.title: How dangerous is Mythos, Anthropic’s new AI model?
item.description: Dario Amodei’s warnings should not be dismissed

item.title: Partner, scapegoat or spoiler? Israel’s place in a fragile ceasefire
item.description: The dealmaking between America and Iran is happening without Binyamin Netanyahu

item.title: Mummified reptiles are revealing how breathing evolved
item.description: The question had previously been up in the air

item.title: AI models could offer mathematicians a common language
item.description: Some hope they will simplify the process of verifying proofs

item.title: How France learned to fight Russian disinformation
item.description: The EU’s most-targeted country is counterattacking against fake news

item.title: Iran and America agree to pause their war
item.description: A two-week truce is a start but questions remain about a more enduring deal

item.title: When emigration helps bad rulers survive
item.description: A new book finds links between mass migration and democratic backsliding worldwide

item.title: Artemis II has offered Earth inspiration
item.description: But rekindled Space Age enthusiasm will need new foundations if it is to endure

item.title: Meet the four Democratic tribes
item.description: What 19,000 survey respondents reveal about rival factions on the left

item.title: Bye, bye to the Trump trades
item.description: Markets are calling time on many Donald-adjacent bets

item.title: Did Israel overestimate the damage to Iran’s missile programme?
item.description: Iran shows no sign of running out

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: A terrifying wait
item.description: Gregg Carlstrom, our Middle East correspondent, on Donald Trump’s blood-curdling threat to erase Iran

item.title: Global democracy is in better shape than you think
item.description: EIU’s annual index suggests an end to the democracy recession

item.title: A wary rapprochement between India and China
item.description: New Indian investment rules are a cautious start

item.title: How the Iran war has sowed panic among farmers
item.description: Soaring prices for fertiliser and fuel could cause a global food shock

item.title: Iran and America are spinning stories about the size of their victories
item.description: The bombast obscures the scale of the damage the war is doing

item.title: As Iran’s civilian economy crumbles, its military economy grows stronger
item.description: War has split the country in two

item.title: The strange, multicultural slang of Toronto’s teenagers
item.description: London rappers have an outsize influence

item.title: As more states legalise gambling, what next for Las Vegas?
item.description: Visitor numbers are down. But legalisation presents new opportunities

item.title: Taiwan’s opposition leader meets Xi Jinping in Beijing
item.description: The trip exposes divisions in her party

item.title: How far do the European Union’s military ambitions go?
item.description: An interview with the EU’s top military officer

item.title: The South American petro-state profiting from the Iran war
item.description: The boom is raising the risk that oil money devours the economy

item.title: European allies are losing hope of keeping America in NATO
item.description: Donald Trump is furious at their refusal to help his war against Iran

item.title: The tumbling rupee could be a big problem for Narendra Modi
item.description: India’s prime minister knows the risks of a weak currency

item.title: Why McDonald’s and KFC are growing like wildfire in China
item.description: Western fast-food brands are expanding into the countryside

item.title: Inflation or recession? The tug of war in bond markets
item.description: Governments’ borrowing costs are being pulled in opposite directions

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Does Donald Trump’s losing streak matter?
item.description: Charlotte Howard, our New York bureau chief, on how to judge the president’s failures

item.title: A captive American in Iran could lead to further escalation
item.description: It could prompt Donald Trump to follow through on his worst threats

item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Pam Bondi’s loyalty only took her so far
item.description: The president wants the impossible from his attorney-general

item.title: Should you take multivitamins?
item.description: Research shows that some people could benefit

item.title: Donald Trump’s approval rating has sunk to Joe Biden’s lowest point
item.description: He is mirroring his predecessor’s post-debate slump of 2024

item.title: Might Hungary’s election sweep away MAGA’s favourite foreign leader?
item.description: Whether Viktor Orban wins or loses, the world’s populists and their opponents are watching

item.title: Lessons for the world from tiny Hungary
item.description: A regime loved by MAGA may soon lose power. That matters

item.title: America’s foes see opportunity in Asia’s oil shock
item.description: Russia and China are quietly stepping in

item.title: Why women, more than men, are abandoning rural Japan
item.description: Bigger cities are, in turn, skewing more female

item.title: How the Gulf’s war is becoming Asia’s crisis too
item.description: Prices, debt, and scarcity will strike a blow against the world’s workshop

item.title: How Africa is changing Catholicism
item.description: The world’s biggest church is becoming less Eurocentric but more unruly

item.title: Demand for autism care is soaring. The system is struggling to cope
item.description: A surge in diagnoses has created a booming industry, riddled with incentives that drive up costs and distort care

item.title: From MAHA to haha
item.description: The paranoid style of medicine is becoming a political liability

item.title: The war with Iran has blown up an America First policy
item.description: What country can afford both a war and the Jones Act?

Business
Politics
Scientists are working on “everything vaccines”
item.description: A single jab could protect against a wide range of pathogens

item.title: A trio of firms want to clean up steelmaking
item.description: The industry is currently responsible for around 8% of the world’s carbon emissions

item.title: A final favour Emmanuel Macron could do for France
item.description: A gracefully timed exit would reset France’s awkward electoral calendar

item.title: On the front lines, Russian soldiers pay officers to stay alive
item.description: The war against Ukraine has created a corrupt economy of blood money

item.title: Will the European Union’s next member come from the north?
item.description: Worried about trade and security, Iceland and Norway are warming to the EU

item.title: A revolution is coming for Germany’s intelligence services
item.description: Derided at home and abroad, Germany’s spooks are about to get sweeping new powers

item.title: How Fox News is luring in Gen Z
item.description: American boomers’ favourite TV network is trying new digital tricks

item.title: The hidden currency of office life
item.description: How status shapes motivation, conflict and career choices

item.title: India’s oil refiners are feeling the squeeze from the Gulf war
item.description: The conflict, and the government, are depleting margins

item.title: War with Iran could accelerate Africa’s oil revival
item.description: Favourable geology and new technologies make it an attractive place to drill

item.title: How the Department of Justice became a feeding ground for MAGA lobbyists
item.description: Allegations are swirling of an influence-peddling operation

item.title: Senegal’s government denies the gravity of its debt crisis
item.description: Its refusal to consider an IMF plan may make things worse

item.title: Iran’s permissive party islands contain hidden dangers
item.description: If American troops turn up, they should not expect a package holiday

item.title: Iran has damaged a surprising amount of American kit
item.description: Losses would be much higher in a war with China

item.description: The longer the war drags on, the less clear are the benefits for ordinary Iranians

item.description: With a Trump-imposed deadline looming, a relative lull in the fighting cannot last much longer

item.description: Never interrupt your enemy when he is making a mistake

item.description: But more than it admits

item.description: Its humbling could raise borrowing costs

item.description: Why some panicky investors are running for the exit

item.title: “Liberation Day” has reshaped trade—but not as Donald Trump hoped
item.description: In many ways, global commerce has strengthened

item.title: Can a country get too rich?
item.description: Norway shows the potential pitfalls of uncommon prosperity

item.description: Even the winners from Donald Trump’s trade war are feeling squeezed

item.description: Donald Trump may send in troops. Does he know what to do with them?

item.title: Hurricane Trump threatens to blow China off course
item.description: Chinese elites do not agree on who may win from the war in Iran

item.title: Index providers should not bend the rules for Elon Musk
item.description: They will only expose ordinary investors to unnecessary risks

item.title: Coal is back in fashion
item.description: An LNG crunch is good news for the world’s dirtiest fuel

item.title: Is China covering up a violent attack at a Beijing market?
item.description: News outlets put a premium on making the country seem harmonious

item.description: Will the Trump administration lose another high-profile case?

item.description: Ahead of an election due in October, a divided opposition and the war could hand him a lifeline

item.description: The president must be aware that the political costs of a land war could be “extremely high”, writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.description: An obsession with GDP is replaced by an obsession with Xi Jinping

item.title: Why a startup is teaching human brain cells to play “Doom”
item.description: They could herald a new type of computing

item.title: After Iran, gold is looking less glittery
item.description: Is the yellow metal the new crypto?

item.title: Donald Trump and the art of bad diplomacy
item.description: One of the advisers the president ignores is his younger self

item.title: The Venezuela Donald Trump “runs” is a land of surreal contrasts
item.description: Animal spirits are stirring. The opposition has hope. The regime’s softening of repression is uneven

item.title: All sides in the Gulf war are at risk of overplaying their hands
item.description: The costs of dragging out the conflict could be very high indeed

item.title: Is Bollywood’s latest megahit propaganda for Narendra Modi?
item.description: “Dhurandhar: The Revenge” portrays India extracting a price for Pakistani perfidy

item.description: America’s regulators and market operators are teaming up to rekindle public listings

item.description: The changes will affect every level. Except one

item.description: China is helping the Revolutionary Guards profit from Iranian crude

item.description: Yemen’s Shia militia could threaten to choke the Red Sea

item.title: Should you track your VO2 max?
item.description: Treat it as a measure, not a target

item.title: The War Room newsletter: The war that shaped modern Iran
item.description: Iran’s conflict with Iraq in the 1980s provides valuable lessons for today, writes Richard Cockett, a senior editor

item.title: Hormuz is not the only weak spot for global trade
item.description: Many shipping routes are vulnerable, from the Strait of Malacca to the Panama Canal

item.title: England has shown the world how to replace farm subsidies
item.description: A rare Brexit dividend

item.title: How Chinese companies are reshaping Indonesia
item.description: They are fleeing cut-throat competition at home

item.title: Millions of Burmese struggle to find safety in Thailand
item.description: Many refugees are left exposed

item.title: China is breaking into one of the world’s weirdest car markets
item.description: An island of American motoring in Central Asia is changing fast

item.title: China’s huge pork industry is a victim of its own success
item.description: The country is awash in its favourite meat

item.title: Does the Iran war increase the risk of a Chinese attack on Taiwan?
item.description: China has reason to wait, but its window of opportunity has widened

item.title: Jiang Shengnan is the most vocal woman in Chinese politics
item.description: She is trying to push change from within the system

item.title: For Donald Trump, Cuba is everything Iran is not
item.description: A breakthrough is in his grasp. But his idea of progress may be a throwback to another era

item.title: Snarled airports and frozen funding test the new DHS secretary
item.description: Markwayne Mullin promises a kinder, gentler department

item.title: America’s spies have a lot to complain about
item.description: They are grappling with politicisation, purges and a president who may not want to hear them

item.title: What the heirs to General Electric did next
item.description: Their success illustrates the benefits of breaking up—and more

item.title: The Iran war has been a stunning aerial success
item.description: Even if, at the political level, its direction is a mess

item.title: A basket of new fruit varieties is coming your way
item.description: Thank gene editing

item.title: China needs a more ambitious growth target
item.description: Otherwise a fourth year of deflation awaits

item.title: The start of the Iran war was determined by spying success
item.description: But even when assassinations come off, the long-term consequences are unclear

item.title: The Iran war in maps and charts
item.description: Visualisations that tell the story of the conflict

item.title: The New President of the United States
item.description: Our report from 1901

item.title: How 250 years of immigration shaped America
item.description: Cycles of openness and restriction are as old as the country itself

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu is the big winner from the Iran war, for now
item.description: To avoid a row with Donald Trump, Western allies are endorsing Israeli theories of war

item.title: Binyamin Netanyahu is the big winner from the Iran war, for now
item.description: To avoid a row with Donald Trump, Western allies are endorsing Israeli theories of war

item.title: Donald Trump bets big on the Iran war
item.description: If it fails to pay off politically, will he cut his losses or double down?

item.title: Why war isn’t always good for defence stocks
item.description: They win only if governments want just enough weapons—but not too many

item.title: The nightmare war scenario is becoming reality in energy markets
item.description: The longer the war in the Gulf, the harsher the global economic fallout

item.title: Are Gulf states running out of missile interceptors?
item.description: Their rulers insist not but they are burning through their stockpiles fast

item.title: The Iran war is a jolt to Dubai’s business model
item.description: Can the emirate keep people and investors coming?

item.title: What France’s new nuclear-arms doctrine means for Europe
item.description: Emmanuel Macron will build more warheads and include allies

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The new cancel culture on campuses
item.description: John Prideaux, our US editor, reports on the pressures facing American universities in the Trump era

item.title: Cuba’s economic divides are widening
item.description: Luck and guile increasingly determine who gets how much

item.title: Why Ali Khamenei may have welcomed the nature of his death
item.description: The ideology of martyrdom could prolong Iran’s regime—and the war

item.title: The Iran war is rapidly engulfing the region
item.description: How far could each side escalate?

item.title: Hizbullah, the reluctant proxy, strikes at Israel
item.description: But the group’s limited actions suggest that it does not want to be in a war that could finish it off

item.title: Can Viktor Orban be beaten?
item.description: We are tracking the polls for Hungary’s election in April

item.title: Data centres in space: less crazy than you think
item.description: They could be cheaper than ones on Earth, with the right technology
